narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we could support—it's not about you as a person, but the patterns it recognized in your profile. Your first-year grades placed you in the bottom decile of your school, which stood out as the biggest signal, suggesting early struggles with the demands of legal coursework that often foreshadow bar challenges. This was reinforced by your third-year decile being in the bottom as well, painting a picture of consistent difficulty keeping pace with peers over time. Your LSAT score of 37 followed closely as a key concern, indicating the analytical skills the model links strongly to bar success might need more sharpening. Even with your high family income, which sometimes provides advantages, it didn't outweigh these academic hurdles in the model's view and actually aligned with patterns where resources haven't always translated to bar passage here. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.5 added to that cautious outlook, showing a foundation that the model sees as somewhat shaky for the rigors ahead. As a white male studying full-time, those demographics and your commitment rounded out a profile that, in combination, tipped the scales toward a 48% predicted chance of bar failure—higher than our threshold for admission. It's a tough combination, like starting a marathon with early stumbles that persist, no matter the support or dedication. The good news is this isn't set in stone; many turn things around with targeted prep, like boosting those decile rankings through focused study habits or LSAT retakes. You've got the drive—targeting law school full-force shows that—and with adjustments, you could rewrite this story. We're rooting for you to prove the model wrong and crush that bar exam. Keep pushing forward.
